serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of green bean cake is approximately 50 g, equivalent to a small palm-sized kuih portion as commonly sold at Singaporean hawker stalls and traditional confectionery shops.

nutrient_source.title: Green Bean Cake — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are accepted as exact HPB source data. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; set to -1 with documentation in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mung Bean-Based Confections — analogous estimate based on mung bean products in the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Singaporean green bean cake (kuih kacang hijau) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 67 is estimated from the closest analogous mung bean-based sweet confection entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 60–76 for cooked sweetened mung bean products. Glycemic load per 50 g serving calculated as (67 × 72.4 × 0.50) / 100 = 24.3 g. Input record GI confidence was 0.4; this estimate is retained at medium confidence pending direct measurement. The input source label 'Embedded GI Database' is not an approved source; the analogous University of Sydney estimate is substituted per Project Veritas source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (31.9 g/100 g) with very low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention despite low direct sodium (24 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit green bean cake to occasional very small portions (≤30 g) because the high sugar load drives insulin-mediated sodium retention, which can elevate blood pressure independently of dietary sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 31.9 g/100 g and total carbohydrate 72.4 g/100 g with negligible fibre (0.7 g/100 g) produce a severe postprandial glucose spike; GI 67 (medium) combined with high carbohydrate density yields a high glycemic load per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit green bean cake; if consumed, restrict to a single small piece (≤20 g) on rare occasions and pair with a protein source to partially attenuate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High refined sugar (31.9 g/100 g) and high total carbohydrate (72.4 g/100 g) with minimal fibre contribute to dyslipidaemia risk through elevated triglycerides; fat content is moderate (4.7 g/100 g) and sodium is low (24 mg/100 g).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should treat green bean cake as an occasional indulgence only, keeping portions to ≤30 g, as the high sugar and refined carbohydrate content can raise triglycerides and contribute to atherogenic dyslipidaemia over time.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar (31.9 g/100 g) and high refined carbohydrate (72.4 g/100 g) with only 0.7 g/100 g fibre promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; no anti-inflammatory nutrients are present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular consumption of green bean cake as its high sugar and refined carbohydrate load promotes systemic inflammation; plain cooked mung beans without added sugar are a suitable anti-inflammatory alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green bean cake (绿豆糕 / Kuih Kacang Hijau) is a traditional Singaporean and Malay sweet confection made primarily from cooked and mashed mung beans (green beans), sugar, and a small amount of oil or vegetable shortening, pressed into decorative moulds and served at room temperature. The texture is dense, smooth, and slightly crumbly, with a mildly sweet, earthy mung bean flavour often enhanced with pandan. It is a popular snack and festive treat found at hawker stalls, traditional confectionery shops, and Malay kuih stalls across Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, green bean cake is energy-dense at 352 kcal per 100 g, driven by a high carbohydrate content of 72.4 g/100 g, of which 31.9 g is sugar. Protein is modest at 5.0 g/100 g and fat is low at 4.7 g/100 g. Dietary fibre is very low at 0.7 g/100 g and sodium is low at 24 mg/100 g. The high sugar and refined carbohydrate load with negligible fibre makes this food unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis except in very small occasional portions. The glycemic index is estimated at 67 (medium), yielding a high glycemic load per standard 50 g serving of approximately 24.3 g. Iron (2.3 mg/100 g) and calcium (12 mg/100 g) are present in modest amounts from the mung bean base.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 with exact match to the named food. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (medium confidence) as no direct entry exists for this specific preparation; this is the only factor preventing full certainty on GI.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional Singaporean green bean cake preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this food as of 2026. No other approved source (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA) provides a sufficiently close match to supply these values without unacceptable estimation error. The input GI source label 'Embedded GI Database' is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2 source policy; it has been replaced with an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (67 × 72.4 × 0.50) / 100 = 24.3 g for a 50 g serving. All core macros (protein_g 5.0, fat_g 4.7, carbohydrate_g 72.4) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for green bean cake / kuih kacang hijau in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT as of 2026.
